title: The Geometric Cost of Normalization: Affine Bounds on the Bayesian Complexity of Neural Networks
abstract:
LayerNorm and RMSNorm impose fundamentally different geometric constraints on their outputs - and this difference has a precise, quantifiable consequence for model complexity. We prove that LayerNorm's mean-centering step, by confining data to a linear hyperplane (through the origin), reduces the Local Learning Coefficient (LLC) of the subsequent weight matrix by exactly $m/2$ (where $m$ is its output dimension); RMSNorm's projection onto a sphere preserves the LLC entirely. This reduction is structurally guaranteed before any training begins, determined by data manifold geometry alone.
  The underlying condition is a geometric threshold: for the codimension-one manifolds we study, the LLC drop is binary -- any non-zero curvature, regardless of sign or magnitude, is sufficient to preserve the LLC, while only affinely flat manifolds cause the drop. At finite sample sizes this threshold acquires a smooth crossover whose width depends on how much of the data distribution actually experiences the curvature, not merely on whether curvature exists somewhere.
  We verify both predictions experimentally with controlled single-layer scaling experiments using the wrLLC framework. We further show that Softmax simplex data introduces a "smuggled bias" that activates the same $m/2$ LLC drop when paired with an explicit downstream bias, proved via the affine symmetry extension of the main theorem and confirmed empirically.

sections.heading: 1Introduction
sections.text:
LayerNorm and RMSNorm are widely treated as interchangeable design choices, but they differ in a structural way that has not previously been quantified: LayerNorm reduces the effective parameter count of any subsequent weight matrix, while RMSNorm does not. We prove this using the Local Learning Coefficient (LLC) [ 16 , 9 ] , a Bayesian measure of a model’s effective complexity: LayerNorm ’s mean-centering step reduces LLC by exactly m / 2 m/2 per normalized layer, a reduction that is structurally guaranteed before training begins. RMSNorm incurs no such cost.

The mechanism is geometric. Normalization layers [ 7 , 1 , 20 ] constrain data to a submanifold of ℝ d \mathbb{R}^{d} , and directions of the weight matrix orthogonal to that manifold become invisible to the loss — genuine symmetries that reduce the LLC. What property of the manifold determines whether this drop occurs? Our answer is a geometric one: whether the manifold is affinely flat. We prove this for single linear layers with squared loss and find experimentally that the threshold is binary — any non-zero curvature, regardless of sign or magnitude, is sufficient to preserve the LLC.

Our contributions are:

• A geometric threshold for LLC reduction. We prove that a weight matrix W ∈ ℝ m × d W\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times d} loses exactly m / 2 m/2 from its LLC whenever the input data lies on a linear hyperplane (through the origin; linear span d s = d − 1 d_{s}=d-1 ) (Section 2.5 ). Experimentally, we find that for codimension-one manifolds, any non-zero curvature is sufficient to prevent this drop (Section 3 ). • A finite-sample phase transition. Experimentally, we find that the observed binary threshold acquires a smooth crossover at finite sample sizes. The width and location of the transition depend on how much of the data distribution actually experiences the curvature, not merely on whether curvature exists somewhere on the manifold (Section 3 ). • Exact LLC predictions for LayerNorm and RMSNorm . LayerNorm confines data to a linear hyperplane (mean-centering sets 𝟏 ⊤ ​ x = 0 \mathbf{1}^{\top}x=0 ), reducing LLC by m / 2 m/2 . RMSNorm projects onto a sphere, which has full linear span, leaving LLC unchanged. We validate both predictions with controlled scaling experiments (Section 4 ). • Simplex-Bias Duality (Proposition 1 ). Softmax simplex data carries a hidden implicit bias ( b smuggled b_{\text{smuggled}} ) that is inert in strictly linear layers ( Δ ​ λ = 0 \Delta\lambda=0 ) but activates an m m -dimensional symmetry and drops LLC by m / 2 m/2 whenever the layer includes an explicit bias (proved theoretically; measured Δ ​ λ = 2.26 ≈ m / 2 = 2.0 \Delta\lambda=2.26\approx m/2=2.0 , robust across seeds). The Post- LayerNorm case is an open problem: the theoretical mechanism is plausible but current SGLD estimation is unreliable for this configuration (Section 2.5 ).

sections.heading: 2.1Singular Learning Theory and the LLC
sections.text: Singular Learning Theory (SLT), developed by Watanabe [ 16 ] and applied to deep learning by Wei et al. [ 17 ] , measures a model’s Bayesian complexity via the Local Learning Coefficient (LLC) — the effective number of parameters as seen by the Bayesian posterior. When the loss has continuous symmetries (directions in parameter space that leave the loss unchanged), the LLC is strictly less than the nominal parameter count. We show that normalization layers introduce such symmetries structurally, before any training occurs.

sections.heading: 2.2Related Work
sections.text:
The geometry of the loss landscape and continuous symmetries in overparameterized networks has been studied from several angles. Şimşek et al. [ 13 ] characterize how permutation symmetries of the network architecture give rise to connected manifolds of equivalent optima, reducing LLC in a manner structurally analogous to our symmetry space S ℳ S_{\mathcal{M}} . Our contribution is orthogonal: we identify a separate, data-manifold-induced source of degeneracy that is independent of the weight-space symmetries of the architecture itself.

Gupta et al. [ 3 ] study the geometric structure of LayerNorm and RMSNorm at inference time; our work differs in connecting normalization geometry to the LLC and deriving exact algebraic predictions.

The LLC has been applied to study training dynamics and phase transitions in Transformers [ 5 ] and to characterize degeneracy in singular models [ 9 ] . Our work complements these by pinpointing a structural—rather than emergent—source of degeneracy arising from normalization operations.

Attention sinks. Xiao et al. [ 19 ] empirically identified sink tokens in autoregressive language models; Ran-Milo [ 11 ] proved their existence is necessary in softmax Transformers; Ruscio et al. [ 12 ] offer a complementary geometric analysis of the sink phenomenon. Our framework suggests a structural interpretation: LayerNorm ’s mean-centering reduces LLC( W V W_{V} ) by m / 2 m/2 , which may structurally bias W V W_{V} toward low-rank solutions associated with sink behavior. We were unable to validate this prediction experimentally (see Conclusion); the connection between LLC reduction and sink formation remains an open question.

sections.heading: 2.3Holonomic Constraints from Normalization
sections.text:
Each normalization operation acts as a holonomic constraint on the data manifold, confining its output to a specific geometric surface. This surface determines the linear span available to the next weight matrix, which in turn determines whether an LLC drop occurs.

• LayerNorm [ 1 ] enforces zero mean and unit variance. The mean-centering step projects data onto the hyperplane { 𝟏 ⊤ ​ x = 0 } \{\mathbf{1}^{\top}x=0\} , a linear subspace of dimension d − 1 d-1 . The variance normalization further confines data to a sphere within this hyperplane, but the linear span of that sphere equals the span of the full hyperplane, so d s = d − 1 d_{s}=d-1 regardless. • RMSNorm [ 20 ] enforces unit RMS without mean-centering. Outputs lie on a sphere { ‖ x ‖ = d } \{\|x\|=\sqrt{d}\} , which contains ± d ​ e i \pm\sqrt{d}\,e_{i} for each standard basis direction e i e_{i} , so span ​ ( S d − 1 ) = ℝ d \mathrm{span}(S^{d-1})=\mathbb{R}^{d} . Linear span: d s = d d_{s}=d .

The span reduction in LayerNorm is the precise condition for an LLC drop; RMSNorm avoids it. We observe in Section 3 and Appendix A that for the manifolds we test, non-zero curvature is sufficient to achieve full linear span, while affinely flat manifolds do not span the full ambient space.

sections.heading: 2.4The Simplex, the Smuggled Bias, and Affine Symmetries
sections.text:
Beyond variance-based normalizations, routing mechanisms like Softmax impose holonomic constraints of a different kind. Softmax projects arbitrary real vectors onto the standard simplex: Σ d − 1 = { x ∈ ℝ d | ∑ i = 1 d x i = 1 , x i > 0 } \Sigma^{d-1}=\left\{x\in\mathbb{R}^{d}\;\middle|\;\sum_{i=1}^{d}x_{i}=1,\>x_{i}>0\right\}

Because the outputs are strictly constrained by 𝟏 T ​ x = 1 \mathbf{1}^{T}x=1 , the data lies on a ( d − 1 ) (d-1) -dimensional hyperplane. To rigorously analyze the degeneracy of a subsequent linear layer W ∈ ℝ m × d W\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times d} , we can apply an affine shift to center this hyperplane at the origin. Let x ′ = x − 1 d ​ 𝟏 x^{\prime}=x-\frac{1}{d}\mathbf{1} . By construction, 𝟏 T ​ x ′ = 0 \mathbf{1}^{T}x^{\prime}=0 , meaning x ′ x^{\prime} resides strictly in a ( d − 1 ) (d-1) -dimensional linear span.

Applying the linear layer W W to the original data yields: W ​ x = W ​ ( x ′ + 1 d ​ 𝟏 ) = W ​ x ′ + ( 1 d ​ W ​ 𝟏 ) Wx=W\left(x^{\prime}+\frac{1}{d}\mathbf{1}\right)=Wx^{\prime}+\left(\frac{1}{d}W\mathbf{1}\right) Let b s ​ m ​ u ​ g ​ g ​ l ​ e ​ d = 1 d ​ W ​ 𝟏 b_{smuggled}=\frac{1}{d}W\mathbf{1} (the row-wise mean of W W ). Thus, W ​ x = W ​ x ′ + b s ​ m ​ u ​ g ​ g ​ l ​ e ​ d Wx=Wx^{\prime}+b_{smuggled} .

This geometric decomposition formalizes three architectural scenarios with distinct degeneracy consequences:

The common thread is that Softmax ’s affine geometry primes the network for degeneracy, with the precise mechanism determined by downstream operations.

sections.heading: 2.5Main Result: Data-Manifold Induced RLCT Reduction
sections.text:
When a weight matrix W ∈ ℝ m × d W\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times d} operates on data confined to a manifold ℳ \mathcal{M} , those directions of W W orthogonal to span ​ ( ℳ ) \mathrm{span}(\mathcal{M}) are invisible to the loss. This “blind spot” is the precise algebraic manifestation of the data-manifold induced singularity in the parameter space.

This setting admits a closed-form RLCT via Watanabe’s formula: the loss is a quadratic polynomial, the zero-loss manifold W 0 W_{0} is smooth and explicitly characterizable, and the Morse-Bott condition holds for any non-degenerate input distribution. The result is an exact algebraic prediction of normalization’s Bayesian cost: for any normalization that acts as a span restriction, the LLC drop is Δ ​ λ = m ​ ( d − d s ) / 2 \Delta\lambda=m(d-d_{s})/2 , determined entirely by the data geometry before any training occurs. We conjecture that the same reduction holds per-layer in deep networks under the wrLLC isolation protocol.

sections.heading: 2.6Supporting Geometric Results
sections.text:
The following results are mathematically straightforward, but we include proof sketches as these exact statements do not appear verbatim in the literature.

Combining the symmetry space dimension (Notation above) and Theorem 2 , we establish the dimension of the constrained weight manifold:

sections.heading: 2.7Generalization to Affine Transformations
sections.text:
The results established above apply to strictly linear weight matrices. However, as demonstrated in Section 2.3, neural network layers often include explicit bias terms or operate on affine manifolds (e.g., f ​ ( x ) = W ​ x + b f(x)=Wx+b ). The extension to the affine case is immediate via homogeneous coordinates:

In particular, Softmax maps its input to the standard simplex, which has affine span d a = d − 1 d_{a}=d-1 , yielding Δ ​ λ = m / 2 \Delta\lambda=m/2 whenever the layer includes an explicit bias. A strictly linear layer ( b = 0 b=0 ) is unaffected, as the simplex’s linear span remains d d .

sections.heading: 3The Geometric Threshold: Curvature vs. Flatness
sections.text: The main theorem proves that affine flatness is sufficient for an LLC drop: a holonomic constraint that eliminates a full dimension of linear span costs exactly m / 2 m/2 from the LLC. This suggests a binary threshold — any non-zero curvature, regardless of sign or magnitude, should be sufficient to preserve the LLC entirely. We test this converse direction by training on manifolds with systematically varied geometry.

sections.heading: 3.1Setup
sections.text: All experiments use a single linear layer W ∈ ℝ m × d W\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times d} , MSE loss with a teacher-student setup, Gaussian inputs mapped onto the target manifold, N = 2000 N=2000 samples, 5 seeds (median reported). Full experimental details are given in Section 4 . We use d = 5 d=5 , m = 4 m=4 throughout, giving a theoretical Δ ​ λ = m / 2 = 2.0 \Delta\lambda=m/2=2.0 for flat manifolds. LLC is estimated via SGLD with the devinterp library [ 14 ] (3 chains, 4000 burn-in + 4000 recorded draws, localization γ = 2 / p \gamma=2/p , lr = 5 × 10 − 4 =5\times 10^{-4} ). We compare the LLC against an unconstrained Gaussian baseline ( λ Gaussian = 12.045 ± 0.283 \lambda_{\text{Gaussian}}=12.045\pm 0.283 ).

sections.heading: 3.2Block A: The Binary Curvature Threshold
sections.text:
We test three qualitatively different curvature regimes, each a ( d − 1 ) (d-1) -dimensional manifold embedded in ℝ d \mathbb{R}^{d} : • Positive curvature: Paraboloid ( x d = ‖ x < d ‖ 2 x_{d}=\|x_{<d}\|^{2} ) and Hyperboloid ( x d = 1 + ‖ x < d ‖ 2 x_{d}=\sqrt{1+\|x_{<d}\|^{2}} ). • Negative curvature: Saddle ( x d = x 1 2 + x 2 2 − x 3 2 − x 4 2 x_{d}=x_{1}^{2}+x_{2}^{2}-x_{3}^{2}-x_{4}^{2} ). • Zero curvature: Flat hyperplane ( x d = 0 x_{d}=0 ).

Results are shown in Table 1 and Figure 1 (Left). All three curved manifolds yield LLC within 0.15 0.15 of the Gaussian baseline, confirming Δ ​ λ ≈ 0 \Delta\lambda\approx 0 . The flat hyperplane drops to 9.939 ± 0.146 9.939\pm 0.146 , giving Δ ​ λ = 2.106 ≈ m / 2 = 2.0 \Delta\lambda=2.106\approx m/2=2.0 . Curvature sign is irrelevant: what matters is only whether the manifold is flat.

sections.heading: 3.3Block B: Finite-Sample Phase Transition
sections.text:
Theory predicts the threshold is sharp (binary). In practice, with finite data and SGLD noise, curvature must exceed a critical scale to register as non-flat. We quantify this by perturbing a flat hyperplane with a Gaussian bump: x d = A ​ exp ⁡ ( − α ​ ‖ x < d ‖ 2 ) x_{d}=A\exp(-\alpha\|x_{<d}\|^{2}) , varying amplitude A A and width α \alpha .

Two regimes (Table 2 , Figure 1 Right): • Wide bump ( α = 0.1 \alpha=0.1 ): curvature is spread across the manifold. LLC rises smoothly with A A , crossing above the flat bound near A ∗ ≈ 0.1 A^{*}\approx 0.1 . • Narrow bump ( α = 10.0 \alpha=10.0 ): curvature is concentrated at the origin; most data points land on the flat region. The LLC remains near the flat bound until A ≥ 1.0 A\geq 1.0 , despite the manifold being theoretically non-flat for any A > 0 A>0 .

This characterizes a limitation of SGLD-based LLC estimation: the sampler is blind to geometric structure below its thermal noise floor. The measured LLC is not just a function of whether curvature exists, but of how much of the data distribution experiences it . Below a critical amplitude that depends on the data–curvature overlap, the sampler cannot distinguish the manifold from flat. The true RLCT remains binary (as the algebraic theory predicts); the smooth crossover is a property of the estimator, not the geometry.

sections.heading: 4Application to Normalization Layers
sections.text: To empirically verify the bounds established in Theorem 1 and Corollary 1 , we must isolate the geometric cost of normalization from the inherent degeneracies of neural networks. The key challenge is that a multi-layer network exposes unbounded continuous symmetries — such as the G ​ L ​ ( r ) GL(r) scaling invariances inherent in adjacent matrix factorizations — which flood the SGLD sampler and make clean LLC estimation intractable. We eliminate this problem by restricting to a minimal single-layer architecture where the only source of degeneracy is the holonomic constraint imposed by the normalization under study.

sections.heading: 4.1Minimal Single-Layer Architecture
sections.text: Our model consists of a single linear layer W ∈ ℝ m × d W\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times d} (no bias) optionally preceded by a normalization operation: f ​ ( x ) = W ⋅ ϕ ​ ( x ) f(x)=W\cdot\phi(x) where ϕ \phi is one of three conditions: (1) Baseline ( ϕ = identity \phi=\text{identity} ), (2) LayerNorm (zero-mean, unit-variance, no learned affine parameters), or (3) RMSNorm (unit-RMS, no learned affine parameters). Because there is only a single weight matrix and no downstream frozen components, W W is the sole source of any structural degeneracy. Any LLC drop measured relative to the Baseline is therefore uniquely attributable to the data manifold’s geometric constraint.

sections.heading: 4.2Teacher-Student Setup
sections.text: We construct a Teacher-Student setup to guarantee a perfectly realizable target, ensuring the student can reach a true global minimum ( L ≈ 0 L\approx 0 ). For each condition, a Teacher model with the same architecture is randomly initialized (weights drawn from 𝒩 ​ ( 0 , 1 ) \mathcal{N}(0,1) ) and frozen. Inputs X ∈ ℝ N × d X\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times d} ( N = 2000 N=2000 ) are drawn from 𝒩 ​ ( 0 , I d ) \mathcal{N}(0,I_{d}) and targets are set to Y = teacher ​ ( X ) Y=\text{teacher}(X) . The Student is then trained on ( X , Y ) (X,Y) via MSE loss until convergence, ensuring it finds the same zero-loss manifold as the Teacher.

sections.heading: 4.3Training and Convergence
sections.text: The Student model is optimized using Adam [ 8 ] with cosine annealing [ 10 ] (initial lr = 10 − 2 =10^{-2} , minimum lr = 10 − 5 =10^{-5} , 1000 epochs, batch size 256). After training, we verify convergence by checking that the final MSE loss is below 10 − 4 10^{-4} . Seeds that fail this criterion are flagged as poorly converged; no such failures were observed in the experiments reported here.

sections.heading: 4.4SGLDλ\lambdaEstimation
sections.text:
After training, we estimate the LLC using SGLD [ 18 ] via the devinterp library [ 14 ] . To keep the per-parameter spring energy constant regardless of model size, we use an adaptive localization γ = 2.0 / ( m ⋅ d ) \gamma=2.0/(m\cdot d) , calibrated to the reference value of γ = 0.1 \gamma=0.1 that works well for p = 20 p=20 parameters (since 2 / 20 = 0.1 2/20=0.1 ). We run 3 chains of 4000 burn-in steps followed by 4000 recorded draws, with SGLD learning rate 5 × 10 − 4 5\times 10^{-4} .

To suppress the occasional divergent chain, we repeat each configuration over 5 independent seeds and report the median Δ ​ λ = λ Baseline − λ norm \Delta\lambda=\lambda_{\text{Baseline}}-\lambda_{\text{norm}} across seeds, together with the standard deviation as a measure of per-seed variance.

sections.heading: 4.5Scaling Experiments
sections.text: We conduct two systematic sweeps to verify the scaling predictions of Corollary 1: • Experiment 1 (vary m m ): Output dimension m ∈ { 2 , 4 , 6 , 8 , 10 } m\in\{2,4,6,8,10\} with input dimension fixed at d = 12 d=12 . Theory predicts Δ ​ λ = m / 2 \Delta\lambda=m/2 . • Experiment 2 (vary d d ): Input dimension d ∈ { 6 , 10 , 14 , 18 } d\in\{6,10,14,18\} with output dimension fixed at m = 4 m=4 . Theory predicts Δ ​ λ = 2.0 \Delta\lambda=2.0 regardless of d d .

sections.heading: 4.6Softmax Degeneracy Experiment
sections.text: To empirically validate Corollary 3 , we evaluate a separate configuration where the input data X X is drawn from the affine hyperplane { x ∈ ℝ d ∣ 𝟏 ⊤ ​ x = 1 } \{x\in\mathbb{R}^{d}\mid\mathbf{1}^{\top}x=1\} rather than a Gaussian. Concretely, each sample is generated as x = z − z ¯ ​ 1 + 1 d ​ 𝟏 x=z-\bar{z}\,\mathbf{1}+\frac{1}{d}\mathbf{1} where z ∼ 𝒩 ​ ( 0 , I ) z\sim\mathcal{N}(0,I) , ensuring 𝟏 ⊤ ​ x = 1 \mathbf{1}^{\top}x=1 while preserving the spread of a standard Gaussian. This captures the essential affine geometry of Softmax outputs (which satisfy the same constraint) while keeping the data variance high enough for reliable LLC estimation. We use the same single-layer architecture ( d = 5 , m = 4 d=5,m=4 ) and compare three downstream configurations: (1) strictly linear ( b = 0 b=0 ), (2) explicit bias ( y = W ​ x + b y=Wx+b ), and (3) LayerNorm post-projection. This isolates whether the simplex geometry triggers the “smuggled bias” degeneracy predicted by the affine symmetry analysis.

sections.heading: 5.1LayerNorm Reduces LLC bym/2m/2
sections.text:
The LLC drop induced by LayerNorm is consistent with the m / 2 m/2 prediction across all tested output dimensions (Figure 2 , Left), in agreement with Corollary 1 . Because LayerNorm mean-centers the data, it projects x x onto the hyperplane { v ∈ ℝ d ∣ 𝟏 ⊤ ​ v = 0 } \{v\in\mathbb{R}^{d}\mid\mathbf{1}^{\top}v=0\} , restricting the linear span to d s = d − 1 d_{s}=d-1 and introducing an m m -dimensional symmetry space in W W . Each lost dimension costs exactly 1 / 2 1/2 from the LLC.

At m = 10 m=10 the estimator breaks down: seeds cluster near 3 3 and 7 7 – 8 8 separately, yielding a median Δ ​ λ = 3.68 ± 2.07 \Delta\lambda=3.68\pm 2.07 against a theoretical prediction of 5.0 5.0 . The bimodal distribution indicates the SGLD sampler is not converging to a single stable estimate but settling into two distinct modes in the loss landscape. The theory predicts Δ ​ λ = 5.0 \Delta\lambda=5.0 ; neither observed mode matches this. This is an estimator failure at larger parameter counts, not a confirmation of the theory; controlled single-layer experiments at this scale would require more chains, longer burn-in, or better-calibrated localization.

Figure 2 (Right) confirms that the drop is independent of input dimension d d : fixing m = 4 m=4 gives Δ ​ λ ≈ 2.0 \Delta\lambda\approx 2.0 for d ∈ { 6 , 10 , 14 , 18 } d\in\{6,10,14,18\} . The LLC reduction depends only on the number of dimensions lost to the constraint, not on the ambient size of the manifold.

sections.heading: 5.2RMSNorm Preserves LLC
sections.text: RMSNorm shows Δ ​ λ ≈ 0 \Delta\lambda\approx 0 across all tested values of m m and d d (Figure 2 ). Without mean-centering, its outputs lie on a hypersphere with d s = d d_{s}=d : W W has no data-induced null space and no continuous symmetry is introduced. This gives a theoretical account of why RMSNorm provides normalization’s training-stability benefits without reducing the effective parameter count of the subsequent weight matrix.

sections.heading: 5.3Softmax and the Smuggled Bias
sections.text:
Results are summarized in Table 3 . With a strictly linear layer ( b = 0 b=0 ), the simplex data yields Δ ​ λ ≈ 0 \Delta\lambda\approx 0 : because the simplex does not pass through the origin, its linear span is d d and no symmetry space exists. The LLC drop is conditional, not automatic.

Adding an explicit bias activates the degeneracy. With y = W ​ x + b y=Wx+b , the measured Δ ​ λ = 2.26 ≈ m / 2 = 2.0 \Delta\lambda=2.26\approx m/2=2.0 : the smuggled bias b smuggled = 1 d ​ W ​ 𝟏 b_{\text{smuggled}}=\frac{1}{d}W\mathbf{1} and the explicit bias b b are indistinguishable to the loss, collapsing m m parameters in accordance with Corollary 3 . This result is robust across seeds (std < 0.5 <0.5 ).

The post-projection LayerNorm case (Conjecture 1 ) is not experimentally resolved: multi-seed estimation gives Δ ​ λ = − 0.92 ± 0.86 \Delta\lambda=-0.92\pm 0.86 , indicating that the SGLD-based Gaussian baseline is poorly calibrated for the non-affine LN loss landscape. We omit that row from the table and leave validation to future work.

sections.heading: 6Conclusion
sections.text:
The central finding is a geometric threshold: whether a normalization operation reduces the LLC of the subsequent weight matrix depends entirely on whether the data manifold is affinely flat, not on any continuous geometric quantity like curvature. LayerNorm confines data to a linear hyperplane ( 𝟏 ⊤ ​ x = 0 \mathbf{1}^{\top}x=0 , d s = d − 1 d_{s}=d-1 ), producing an LLC drop of m / 2 m/2 (proved for single linear layers with squared loss); RMSNorm projects onto a sphere, which has full linear span, producing no drop. For the codimension-one manifolds we study, any non-zero curvature is sufficient to preserve the LLC — the distinction appears binary, not graded.

This binary threshold has a smooth finite-sample counterpart. The effective RLCT measured by SGLD depends not just on whether curvature exists, but on how much of the data distribution actually experiences it. Wide, distributed curvature restores the LLC at amplitudes an order of magnitude smaller than narrow, concentrated curvature — even when both are theoretically non-flat. This is an intrinsic feature of SGLD-based LLC estimation, not a failure of the algebraic theory; the true RLCT remains binary.

The wrLLC methodology makes the geometric cost of normalization precise and measurable in a single-layer experiment. Under the conditions of Theorem 1 (single linear layer, squared loss), LayerNorm reduces Δ ​ λ = m / 2 \Delta\lambda=m/2 for any non-degenerate input distribution; RMSNorm preserves LLC by projecting onto a full-rank sphere; Softmax induces the same m / 2 m/2 drop when paired with an explicit bias (proved, experimentally confirmed); the Post- LayerNorm case is a theoretical open problem. A natural open question is whether the LLC drop in W V W_{V} under Pre- LayerNorm manifests as stronger attention sinks in practice. Our preliminary experiments on a small attention-only transformer found no significant difference between LayerNorm and RMSNorm conditions, suggesting this connection, if it exists, may require larger models or tasks that more directly expose the low-rank structure of W V W_{V} .

A further direction concerns grokking dynamics : by removing degrees of freedom associated with memorization, LayerNorm ’s LLC reduction may make the low-LLC generalizing circuit more accessible during training, potentially accelerating the grokking transition relative to RMSNorm or unnormalized baselines.

sections.heading: Local Curvature vs. Global Homology
sections.text:
Section 3 shows empirically that local curvature is sufficient to preserve the LLC: all curved manifolds tested (paraboloid, hyperboloid, saddle) matched the Gaussian baseline, while the flat hyperplane dropped by Δ ​ λ ≈ m / 2 = 2.0 \Delta\lambda\approx m/2=2.0 (Table 1 ). Among the manifolds tested, curvature sign is irrelevant; only flatness matters.

However, the Block B experiment (Table 2 ) shows that local curvature and global homology differ sharply in their robustness at finite sample sizes . When curvature is small or concentrated, the SGLD sampler cannot distinguish a weakly curved manifold from a flat one: the effective RLCT collapses to the flat bound even when the manifold is theoretically non-flat. This is a finite-sample phase transition in the estimated RLCT, not a failure of the algebraic theory.

Global non-trivial homology (as in S d − 1 S^{d-1} ) is immune to this effect. A closed orientable hypersurface with non-trivial top-homology separates ℝ d \mathbb{R}^{d} into two components and cannot be embedded in any proper hyperplane — unlike a curved but non-closed patch, which could in principle be continuously flattened. Furthermore, because normalization operations fix the macroscopic scale of the data (standardizing to unit RMS), the topological volume is locked well above the thermal noise floor of the SGLD sampler. We conjecture that non-trivial top-homology therefore provides an unconditional, scale-independent guarantee of LLC preservation, while local curvature provides only a conditional guarantee that breaks down at finite amplitude. We leave a formal proof of this conjecture to future work.

figures.caption: Figure 1: Curvature geometry and effective LLC. Left: Median LLC per manifold class ( d = 5 d=5 , m = 4 m=4 , 5 seeds). All curved surfaces match the Gaussian baseline ( ≈ 12.0 \approx 12.0 ); only the flat hyperplane drops to ≈ 9.9 ≈ 12.0 − m / 2 \approx 9.9\approx 12.0-m/2 . Curvature sign is irrelevant: the threshold is between flat and non-flat. Right: LLC vs. bump amplitude A A for wide ( α = 0.1 \alpha=0.1 ) and narrow ( α = 10.0 \alpha=10.0 ) bumps. The wide bump recovers near A ∗ ≈ 0.1 A^{*}\approx 0.1 ; the narrow bump stays near the flat bound until A ≥ 1.0 A\geq 1.0 , showing that the effective RLCT depends on data–curvature overlap, not just the existence of curvature.

Figure 2: LLC reduction under LayerNorm and RMSNorm . Left: Varying output dimension m m with fixed d = 12 d=12 . Δ ​ λ \Delta\lambda is consistent with the m / 2 m/2 prediction for LayerNorm (with increasing variance at large m m ); RMSNorm shows Δ ​ λ ≈ 0 \Delta\lambda\approx 0 throughout. Right: Varying input dimension d d with fixed m = 4 m=4 . Δ ​ λ ≈ 2.0 \Delta\lambda\approx 2.0 regardless of d d , confirming that the drop depends only on the number of dimensions lost to the constraint.
